began on April 17, 1939. The regular season ended on October 1, with the [Cincinnati Reds](/wiki/Cincinnati_Reds "Cincinnati Reds") and [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") as the regular season champions of the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") and [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League"), respectively. The postseason began with Game 1 of the [36th World Series](/wiki/1939_World_Series "1939 World Series") on October 4 and ended with Game 4 on October 8. The Yankees swept the Reds in four games, capturing their eighth championship in franchise history, and their last in a four-World Series run, becoming the first team to win four consecutive World Series.

The **1939 major league baseball season** began on April 17, 1939. The regular season ended on October 1, with the [Cincinnati Reds](/wiki/Cincinnati_Reds "Cincinnati Reds") and [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") as the regular season champions of the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") and [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League"), respectively. The postseason began with Game 1 of the [36th World Series](/wiki/1939_World_Series "1939 World Series") on October 4 and ended with Game 4 on October 8. The Yankees swept the Reds in four games, capturing their eighth championship in franchise history, and their last in a four-World Series run, becoming the first team to win four consecutive World Series.

The [seventh Major League Baseball All-Star Game](/wiki/1939_Major_League_Baseball_All-Star_Game "1939 Major League Baseball All-Star Game") was held on July 11 at [Yankee Stadium](/wiki/Yankee_Stadium_(1923) "Yankee Stadium (1923)") in [New York](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), home of the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees"). The [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League") won, 3–1.

## Schedule

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1939_Major_League_Baseball_season&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Schedule")]

See also: [Major League Baseball schedule](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_schedule "Major League Baseball schedule")

The 1939 schedule consisted of 154 games for all teams in the American League and National League, each of which had eight teams. Each team was scheduled to play 22 games against the other seven teams of their respective league. This continued the format put in place since the [1904](/wiki/1904_Major_League_Baseball_season "1904 Major League Baseball season") season (except for [1919](/wiki/1919_Major_League_Baseball_season "1919 Major League Baseball season")) and would be used until [1961](/wiki/1961_Major_League_Baseball_season "1961 Major League Baseball season") in the American League and [1962](/wiki/1962_Major_League_Baseball_season "1962 Major League Baseball season") in the National League.

National League Opening Day took place on April 17 with a game between the [Pittsburgh Pirates](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Pirates "Pittsburgh Pirates") and [Cincinnati Reds](/wiki/Cincinnati_Reds "Cincinnati Reds"), while American League Opening Day took place on the following day, with a game between the [Chicago White Sox](/wiki/Chicago_White_Sox "Chicago White Sox") and [Detroit Tigers](/wiki/Detroit_Tigers "Detroit Tigers"). This continued the trend from the [previous season](/wiki/1938_Major_League_Baseball_season "1938 Major League Baseball season") which saw both leagues opened on different days. It was the first season since [1901](/wiki/1901_Major_League_Baseball_season "1901 Major League Baseball season") that saw both leagues open with just one game each. The final day of the regular season was on October 1 and featured twelve teams. The [World Series](/wiki/1939_World_Series "1939 World Series") took place between October 4 and October 8.

## Rule change

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1939_Major_League_Baseball_season&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Rule change")]

The 1939 season saw the following rule change:

* The scoring sacrifice fly, exempting a batter from a time at bat when a runner scored after the putout on a fly ball, was restored for the first time since its elimination in [1931](/wiki/1931_Major_League_Baseball_season "1931 Major League Baseball season"). This would last only one season.[[1]](#cite_note-1)
* Similar to the [infield fly rule](/wiki/Infield_fly_rule "Infield fly rule"), now an umpire could make a judgement about an outfield fly ball. If it is judged that an [outfielder](/wiki/Outfielder "Outfielder") intentionally drops a fly ball or line drive, the umpire shall immediately rule the ball has been caught. Like the infield fly rule, this only applies when there are less than two outs, and runners occupying at least first and second base.[[2]](#cite_note-1938_Winter_SABR-2)
* [Pitchers](/wiki/Pitcher "Pitcher") can now start with only his pivot foot on or making contact in front of the [pitching rubber](/wiki/Pitching_rubber "Pitching rubber").[[2]](#cite_note-1938_Winter_SABR-2)
* In the [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League"):
  + [Night games](/wiki/Night_game#Baseball "Night game") were now approved, with up to seven night games allowed per team, though teams could not play at night on Saturdays, Sundays, holidays, nor could they start an inning after 11:50 p.m..[[2]](#cite_note-1938_Winter_SABR-2)
  + Baseballs would now match the [National League's](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") stitching.[[2]](#cite_note-1938_Winter_SABR-2)

## Milestones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1939_Major_League_Baseball_season&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Milestones")]

### Batters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1939_Major_League_Baseball_season&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Batters")]

#### Two grand slams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1939_Major_League_Baseball_season&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Two grand slams")]

See also: [List of Major League Baseball single-game grand slam leaders](/wiki/List_of_Major_League_Baseball_single-game_grand_slam_leaders "List of Major League Baseball single-game grand slam leaders")

* [Jim Tabor](/wiki/Jim_Tabor "Jim Tabor") ([BOS](/wiki/Boston_Red_Sox "Boston Red Sox")):
  + Became the second player to hit two [grand slams](/wiki/Grand_slam_(baseball) "Grand slam (baseball)") in a single game, in an 18–12 win over the [Philadelphia Athletics](/wiki/Philadelphia_Athletics "Philadelphia Athletics").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

#### Cycles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1939_Major_League_Baseball_season&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Cycles")]

See also: [List of Major League Baseball players to hit for the cycle](/wiki/List_of_Major_League_Baseball_players_to_hit_for_the_cycle "List of Major League Baseball players to hit for the cycle")

* [Sam Chapman](/wiki/Sam_Chapman "Sam Chapman") ([PHA](/wiki/Philadelphia_Athletics "Philadelphia Athletics")):
  + Chapman [hit for his first cycle](/wiki/Hitting_for_the_cycle "Hitting for the cycle") and ninth in franchise history, on May 5 against the [St. Louis Browns](/wiki/St._Louis_Browns "St. Louis Browns").[[9]](#cite_note-9)
* [Charlie Gehringer](/wiki/Charlie_Gehringer "Charlie Gehringer") ([DET](/wiki/Detroit_Tigers "Detroit Tigers")):
  + Gehringer hit for his first cycle, the fourth in franchise history, and fifth natural cycle in major league history on May 27 against the [St. Louis Browns](/wiki/St._Louis_Browns "St. Louis Browns").[[10]](#cite_note-10)
* [Arky Vaughan](/wiki/Arky_Vaughan "Arky Vaughan") ([PIT](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Pirates "Pittsburgh Pirates")):
  + Vaughan hit for his second cycle, the 11th in franchise history, and the fifth reverse cycle in major league history on July 19 against the [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants_(baseball) "New York Giants (baseball)").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Miscellaneous

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1939_Major_League_Baseball_season&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Miscellaneous")]

* [Lou Gehrig](/wiki/Lou_Gehrig "Lou Gehrig") ([NYY](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees")):
  + Set a Major League record for [most consecutive games](/wiki/List_of_Major_League_Baseball_consecutive_games_played_leaders "List of Major League Baseball consecutive games played leaders") at 2,130 on April 30. It would be the final game of his career.

## Venues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1939_Major_League_Baseball_season&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Venues")]

Over 77 home games, the [Cleveland Indians](/wiki/Cleveland_Guardians "Cleveland Guardians") played 47 games at League Park and 30 games at Cleveland Stadium.[[32]](#cite_note-32) This would be the 5th of 12 seasons since [1932](/wiki/1932_Major_League_Baseball_season "1932 Major League Baseball season") that saw the Indians play at both venues.

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1939_Major_League_Baseball_season&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Media")]

### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1939_Major_League_Baseball_season&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Television")]

The first Major League game is [televised](/wiki/Television "Television") on August 26, when WXBS-TV broadcasts the game between the [Cincinnati Reds](/wiki/Cincinnati_Reds "Cincinnati Reds") and the [Brooklyn Dodgers](/wiki/Brooklyn_Dodgers "Brooklyn Dodgers") at [Ebbets Field](/wiki/Ebbets_Field "Ebbets Field").[[33]](#cite_note-Pellowski-33) The two teams played a [doubleheader](/wiki/Doubleheader_(baseball) "Doubleheader (baseball)") that day, and the second game was also televised.

## Retired numbers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1939_Major_League_Baseball_season&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Retired numbers")]

* [Lou Gehrig](/wiki/Lou_Gehrig "Lou Gehrig") had his No. 4 retired by the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") on July 4. This was the first number retired by the team and first retired number in MLB history.
